quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 4 Quiz 1
quiz.subtitle: Scalene, Isosceles, Equilateral; Acute, Right, Obtuse, Equiangular
quiz.subject: Geometry
quiz.topic: Geometry: 4.1 Classifying Triangles by Sides and Angles

questions.question: A triangle has three sides of lengths $5$ cm, $5$ cm, and $8$ cm. How is it classified by its sides?
questions.explanation: An isosceles triangle has at least two congruent sides. With two sides of $5$ cm, this triangle is isosceles.

questions.question: A triangle has three sides of lengths $7$ cm, $7$ cm, and $7$ cm. How is it classified by its sides?
questions.explanation: An equilateral triangle has all three sides congruent. With all sides measuring $7$ cm, this triangle is equilateral.

questions.question: A triangle has sides of lengths $4$ cm, $6$ cm, and $9$ cm. How is it classified by its sides?
questions.explanation: A scalene triangle has no congruent sides. All three side lengths are different, so this triangle is scalene.

questions.question: A triangle has angles measuring $30^\circ$, $60^\circ$, and $90^\circ$. How is it classified by its angles?
questions.explanation: A right triangle has one angle that measures exactly $90^\circ$. The $90^\circ$ angle makes this a right triangle.

questions.question: A triangle has angles measuring $35^\circ$, $55^\circ$, and $90^\circ$. How is it classified by its angles?
questions.explanation: The presence of a $90^\circ$ angle classifies this as a right triangle. The other two angles are acute, but the defining classification is right.

questions.question: A triangle has angles measuring $40^\circ$, $70^\circ$, and $70^\circ$. How is it classified by both sides and angles?
questions.explanation: Two equal angles of $70^\circ$ mean the sides opposite them are congruent, making it isosceles. All angles are less than $90^\circ$, so it is acute.

questions.question: A triangle has angles measuring $20^\circ$, $40^\circ$, and $120^\circ$. How is it classified by its angles?
questions.explanation: An obtuse triangle has one angle greater than $90^\circ$. The $120^\circ$ angle makes this an obtuse triangle.

questions.question: A triangle has angles measuring $60^\circ$, $60^\circ$, and $60^\circ$. How is it classified by both sides and angles?
questions.explanation: All three angles are $60^\circ$, making it equiangular. Every equiangular triangle is also equilateral, with all three sides congruent.

questions.question: An isosceles triangle has a vertex angle of $40^\circ$. What must be true about its base angles?
options: One base angle is $40^\circ$ and the other is $100^\circ$
The base angles cannot be determined
questions.explanation: The sum of angles in a triangle is $180^\circ$. With a vertex angle of $40^\circ$, the two base angles sum to $140^\circ$. Since base angles of an isosceles triangle are congruent, each measures $70^\circ$.

questions.question: Can a triangle be both right and equilateral?
options: No, because an equilateral triangle has all angles equal to $60^\circ$
Yes, because a right angle is $90^\circ$ and the others can sum to $90^\circ$
Yes, because a triangle can have a right angle and three equal sides
No, because a right triangle must be isosceles
questions.explanation: An equilateral triangle has all three angles measuring $60^\circ$. A right triangle requires one $90^\circ$ angle, which is impossible if all angles are $60^\circ$. So a triangle cannot be both right and equilateral.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You can confidently classify triangles by both side lengths and angle measures, and understand the relationships between them.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand the main classifications but review the connection between side classifications and angle classifications in isosceles and equilateral triangles.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: by sides — scalene (no equal sides), isosceles (at least two equal sides), equilateral (all three equal); by angles — acute (all $<$ $90^\circ$), right (one $90^\circ$), obtuse (one $>$ $90^\circ$), equiangular (all $60^\circ$).
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Triangle classifications: scalene/isosceles/equilateral describe sides; acute/right/obtuse/equiangular describe angles. An equilateral triangle is always equiangular, and vice versa.
